text: <par>The trigger is, as it were, the navel of a gun or crossbow. See Matisoff 1988, <emph>The Dictionary of Lahu</emph>, p. 1373.</par>
<par>Contra L.D. p.895, this is NOT a loan from Thai.</par>

<par>This Lahu form probably descends from PLB *<latinform>ʔgyuk</latinform>; the high rising tone points to a doubly-glottalized prototype, while the initial <latinform>c-</latinform> is a regular reflex of PLB <reconstruction>*gy-</reconstruction>.</par>

<par>cf. Lahu <latinform>na, na-e</latinform> ‘get better’ DL p.727-728; perhaps from evantidramic root <reconstruction>*na</reconstruction> ‘good’ or causative of <latinform>nà</latinform> ‘make hurt go away.’ </par>
<par>Second syllable derived tentatively from <reconstruction>*ʔmik</reconstruction> (see DL p. 1038). Cf. Achang Xiandao for same sequence of morphemes.</par>
<par>Second syllable derived tentatively from <reconstruction>*ʔmik</reconstruction> (see DL p. 1038).</par>
<par>This etymon is mistakenly reconstructed as Proto-Loloish <reconstruction>*baw³</reconstruction> in Bradley 1979 (DB-PL) #597, on the basis of WB <latinform>paw</latinform> 'to appear'.</par>
<par>The immediate ancestor of Lahu <latinform>qá-</latinform> would be PLB <latinform>*ʔgak</latinform>.</par>
<par>Literally "vagina-nipple". The last syllable probably means 'red'. Cf. also Lahu <latinform>ha-cú-ni</latinform> 'uvula' (lit. "tongue-nipple").</par>
<par>Lahu <latinform>bà</latinform> means 'throw away' in isolation, but as an auxiliary verb means 'to perform an action so that something is gotten rid of'. See HPTB, p. 231.</par>

<par>This word was originally glossed 'whole arm (shoulder to wrist)' during JAM's 1965-66 field trip, but was revised to 'narrow part of forearm where it joins the hand' when the data was revised on subsequent trips (cf. <latinform>qɔ̄</latinform> 'neck'). Given the imprecision of body part terminology, it is very possible that both glosses are correct.</par>
<par>Lahu <latinform>šɨ</latinform> is an "extentive" noun derived from the adjectival verb <latinform>yɨ̀</latinform>. For the morphophonemics of exentive nouns, see Matisoff 1973/1982: 30-32.</par>

<par>This word has been grammaticalized to a locative particle, and no longer retains its full nominal meaning. It reflects PLB Tone *3, while cognates like WB <latinform>lâm</latinform> reflect Tone *2.</par>
<par>The high rising tone in this Lahu form indicates an earlier *pre-glottalized initial, perhaps PLB <reconstruction>*ʔdzwatᴸ</reconstruction>.</par>
<par>Lahu <latinform>yō(n)</latinform> is a borrowing from Burmese, probably via Shan.</par>
<par>This Lahu form seems to be a fusion of the bare root <latinform>*ka</latinform> plus a second element meaning GO (see <xref ref="657">#657</xref>).</par>
